race.name: Amy's Gran Fondo
race.tagline: 103 km along Australia's Great Ocean Road. A ride built on memory — honoring Amy Gillett, the cyclist killed in 2005 whose legacy reshaped cycling safety in Australia.

vitals.location: Lorne to Aireys Inlet, Great Ocean Road, Victoria, Australia
vitals.registration: Online via amysgranfondo.org.au. AUD $100-150 (~$65-100 USD) depending on distance and registration period.
vitals.prize_purse: None. Charity ride supporting the Amy Gillett Foundation.
vitals.aid_stations: Feed zones along the Great Ocean Road and through the Otway Ranges. Water, electrolytes, fruit, and energy food. Medical support at key points.
vitals.cutoff_time: Generous cutoff. Full course support and SAG vehicles.

climate.description: September in coastal Victoria is Australian spring — unpredictable and often dramatic. Temperatures of 55-70F (12-21C) with the Southern Ocean pushing weather systems through rapidly. The Great Ocean Road is exposed to coastal wind and weather. Cloud, sun, rain, and wind can cycle through in a single hour. Cool mornings are common. The Otway Ranges can be cooler and wetter than the coast.
challenges: Variable spring weather — sun, rain, wind can change rapidly
Coastal wind exposure along the Great Ocean Road
Otway Ranges can be cooler and wetter than the coastal sections

terrain.primary: Great Ocean Road coastal and Otway Ranges climbing
terrain.surface: Paved roads throughout — the Great Ocean Road is well-maintained. Coastal riding with ocean views, then inland climbs through the Otway Ranges temperate rainforest. Rolling with several sharp ascents. The Great Ocean Road hugs the coast with curves and elevation changes. Otway climbs are forested and atmospheric.
features: Great Ocean Road — one of the world's most scenic coastal roads
Otway Ranges climbs through temperate rainforest

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Australia's memorial ride — honoring Amy Gillett on the Great Ocean Road. The emotional weight and scenic beauty create an experience that transcends the modest physical challenge. T3 by score (56).
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Experience=4 (the cause and the scenery combine for genuine emotional impact). Community=4 (strong charity community united by cycling safety advocacy). Prestige=3 (Amy Gillett's memory carries deep meaning in Australian cycling). Length=2 and elevation=2 reflect a moderate physical challenge.

logistics.explanation: Melbourne (Tullamarine) is Australia's second-largest airport with domestic and international connections. Lorne is about 2.5 hours southwest via the Great Ocean Road or the faster inland route. Event logistics are solid — well-organized by the Amy Gillett Foundation with clear information, bus transfers, and parking. The Great Ocean Road infrastructure supports the event well.

length.explanation: 103 km (64 miles) is a medio-gran fondo distance by European standards. With 1,500m of climbing, this is a 3-5 hour ride for most competent cyclists. Not a century, not a major endurance challenge. The 60 km option is an honest half-day ride. The distance is accessible, which aligns with the event's inclusive, charity-focused mission.

technicality.explanation: Paved roads throughout. The Great Ocean Road has curves and the Otway climbs require basic descending skills, but nothing technically demanding. This is a ride anyone with basic road cycling competence can complete safely.

elevation.explanation: 1,500 meters of climbing across 103 km is moderate. The Otway Ranges provide real climbing — sustained forest ascents that will tax untrained legs — but nothing extreme. The Great Ocean Road coastal sections add rolling terrain. Enough vertical to make it a proper ride, not enough to make it a mountain challenge.

climate.explanation: September in Victoria is spring — variable but not extreme. The weather can change rapidly along the Great Ocean Road, and coastal wind is a factor. But temperatures are comfortable for cycling (55-70F), and genuine extremes are rare. The climate adds character rather than danger. Bring a vest, be prepared for rain, ride through it.

altitude.explanation: The Great Ocean Road is at sea level. Otway Ranges peaks are modest. Altitude is completely irrelevant.

adventure.explanation: The Great Ocean Road is one of the world's iconic coastal drives, and riding it on a bike is special. The Otway Ranges temperate rainforest is lush and atmospheric. Southern Ocean views, koala sightings, towering eucalyptus. The scenery is genuinely beautiful. But this is well-traveled tourist infrastructure, not remote adventure.

prestige.explanation: Amy Gillett's name carries deep significance in Australian cycling. She was an Olympic rower turned cyclist killed by a motorist while training in Germany in 2005. Her death catalyzed cycling safety advocacy across Australia. The foundation bearing her name has driven 'a metre matters' legislation. Riding in her memory has genuine emotional weight that goes beyond typical event prestige.

race_quality.explanation: Well-organized charity event. The Amy Gillett Foundation manages logistics professionally. Feed zones are adequate, course marking is clear, medical support is present. But this is a charity ride, not a premium gran fondo — expect solid but not lavish organization. The focus is on the cause, not the production value.

experience.explanation: This is where Amy's Gran Fondo exceeds its modest physical challenge. Riding the Great Ocean Road in Amy Gillett's memory, surrounded by 3,000-4,000 riders who understand why cycling safety matters, creates genuine emotional resonance. The scenery — Southern Ocean, Otway rainforest, coastal cliffs — provides the visual backdrop. The cause provides the meaning. Finishing isn't just about fitness; it's about riding for something larger.

community.explanation: The Amy Gillett Foundation has built a passionate community around cycling safety advocacy. Riders include survivors of road incidents, families affected by cycling fatalities, and thousands who simply believe in the cause. The shared mission creates deeper bonds than a typical gran fondo. The post-ride atmosphere is warm, emotional, and genuine. This community rides for each other.

field_depth.explanation: Participatory charity ride. The front group includes strong Australian club riders, but competitive racing is not the point. The depth is in the community breadth — from serious cyclists to occasional riders — not the racing pedigree. If you want to race, this is the wrong event.

value.explanation: AUD $100-150 (~$65-100 USD) is reasonable for a well-organized charity ride with full course support. A significant portion of fees supports the Amy Gillett Foundation's cycling safety work. You're paying for the ride and contributing to the cause. Fair value, not exceptional value.

expenses.explanation: Lorne and the Great Ocean Road towns are tourist destinations — accommodation isn't cheap, especially in September when spring tourism picks up. Hotels/Airbnbs run AUD $150-250/night. Melbourne accommodation is more affordable with a longer drive to the start. Food along the Great Ocean Road is tourist-priced. Budget AUD $600-1,000 for a long weekend.

biased_opinion.summary: Amy's Gran Fondo is not the hardest ride in Australia, the longest, or the most technically demanding. It's something rarer: a ride with genuine meaning. Amy Gillett was an Olympic rower turned cyclist, killed at 29 by a motorist while training in Germany. Her death shook Australian cycling and sparked a movement — the Amy Gillett Foundation has driven 'a metre matters' legislation and cycling safety awareness across the country. The gran fondo rides the Great Ocean Road in her memory, and 3,000-4,000 cyclists show up each year not just to ride, but to ride for something. The course is beautiful — Southern Ocean coastline, Otway rainforest, koalas in eucalyptus trees. But the beauty that matters most is the purpose.
strengths: The cause: riding in memory of Amy Gillett and supporting cycling safety has genuine emotional weight
Great Ocean Road: one of the world's great coastal roads, experienced from the saddle
Community: 3,000-4,000 riders united by a cause deeper than fitness goals
Accessibility: 103 km with moderate climbing welcomes riders of all abilities
weaknesses: Modest physical challenge: 103 km / 1,500m won't test strong cyclists
Weather lottery: Victorian spring on the coast can deliver anything from sunshine to sideways rain
Not a race: if you want competition, the charity-ride format won't deliver that edge
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A ride where the reason you're riding matters more than how fast you finish — and the Great Ocean Road makes sure you never forget where you are.

final_verdict.one_liner: 103 km along the Great Ocean Road in memory of Amy Gillett. Where cycling safety has a heartbeat.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to ride the Great Ocean Road for a cause that matters to every cyclist who shares the road with cars. Yes if you want a beautiful, accessible ride with a passionate community. Skip if you need serious physical challenge or competitive racing.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want an Australian gran fondo with more climbing: try the 3 Peaks Challenge. If you want Great Ocean Road cycling without the event: ride it yourself any day. If you want a charity cycling event in North America: try the Gran Fondo Whistler.

logistics.airport: Melbourne Tullamarine (MEL) — international and domestic connections. Lorne is approximately 2.5 hours southwest. Avalon Airport (AVV) — budget domestic flights, closer to Geelong and the Great Ocean Road.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Lorne is a small coastal town — accommodation is limited and books out fast for event weekend. Book months in advance. Apollo Bay and Aireys Inlet are nearby alternatives. Melbourne accommodation is abundant but means a 2.5-hour drive. Budget AUD $150-250/night in Lorne. Many riders stay in Geelong (1 hour from Lorne) for more options.
logistics.food: Great Ocean Road towns have solid cafe and restaurant scenes — Australian coastal cuisine with good coffee. Lorne's main strip has multiple options. Carb loading: find a pasta or fish-and-chips spot. Post-ride: coffee and a pie from a local bakery. Australian cycling culture runs on flat whites.
logistics.packet_pickup: Saturday before the event in Lorne. Registration and event information from the Amy Gillett Foundation.
logistics.parking: Lorne has limited parking — arrive early or use event shuttle options from Geelong or Melbourne. Follow event transport directions.

history.origin_story: Created in memory of Amy Gillett, an Australian Olympic rower who transitioned to competitive cycling and was killed at age 29 by a motorist while training with the Australian national cycling team on a road in Zeulenroda, Germany, on July 18, 2005. Six of her teammates were also hit — all survived with serious injuries. Her death galvanized Australian cycling safety advocacy. The Amy Gillett Foundation was established to reduce the incidence of cycling fatalities, and the gran fondo became its flagship fundraising event, using the Great Ocean Road as a stage for both remembrance and the message that every cyclist deserves safe passage.
notable_moments: 2005: Amy Gillett killed by a motorist while training in Germany, catalyzing Australian cycling safety movement
Amy Gillett Foundation established, driving 'a metre matters' minimum passing distance legislation
~2011: First Amy's Gran Fondo on the Great Ocean Road
Event has grown to 3,000-4,000 riders, becoming Australia's premier charity cycling event
history.reputation: Australia's memorial ride. Where the Great Ocean Road meets a cause worth riding for.

course_description.character: Coastal beauty and forested climbing along one of the world's great roads. The route starts in Lorne and rides the Great Ocean Road — Southern Ocean on one side, Otway Ranges forest on the other. The coastal sections are rolling with curves that hug the cliffs. Inland diversions climb through temperate rainforest — tall eucalyptus, tree ferns, dappled light. The route finishes in Aireys Inlet. The terrain is moderate — challenging enough to feel earned, accessible enough that the ride's meaning reaches everyone.

suffering_zones.label: Lorne Start
suffering_zones.desc: Rolling out from Lorne, one of the Great Ocean Road's most charming coastal towns. The Southern Ocean stretches to the horizon. Morning light on the water. The emotional weight of riding in Amy's memory is palpable at the start line.

suffering_zones.label: The Otway Climbs
suffering_zones.desc: The route turns inland into the Otway Ranges. Temperate rainforest climbing — tall eucalyptus, tree ferns, damp air. The climbs are sustained but moderate in gradient. The canopy closes overhead. This is where the Great Ocean Road's tourism gives way to something quieter and greener.

suffering_zones.label: The Coastal Return
suffering_zones.desc: Back on the Great Ocean Road. The Southern Ocean reappears. Rolling coastal terrain with curves that reward smooth bike handling. Wind can be a factor on exposed headland sections. The scenery — coastal cliffs, crashing waves, distant headlands — is the Great Ocean Road at its best.

suffering_zones.label: Aireys Inlet Finish
suffering_zones.desc: The final approach to Aireys Inlet. The iconic Split Point Lighthouse marks the finish. The last kilometers are rolling and scenic. The finish line atmosphere is emotional — riders who lost friends to road incidents, families honoring loved ones, and thousands who rode for the cause.

course_description.signature_challenge: The Great Ocean Road itself — not a single climb or feature, but 103 km of coastal beauty and Otway forest riding that serves as a stage for remembrance.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Great Ocean Road coastal roads and Otway Ranges forest climbs
race_challenges: Variable Victorian spring weather — be prepared for rain and wind
Coastal wind exposure along the Great Ocean Road
Otway Ranges climbing through temperate rainforest
Emotional weight of the event can affect pacing and focus

non_negotiables.requirement: Layering strategy for variable coastal spring weather
non_negotiables.why: Victorian spring on the Great Ocean Road can deliver sunshine, rain, and wind in the same hour. Bring a packable rain vest, arm warmers, and a base layer that works wet or dry. You may need all of them. You may need none of them. Be prepared for both.

non_negotiables.requirement: Endurance base for 103 km with 1,500m climbing
non_negotiables.why: The distance is moderate but the Otway climbs and coastal wind make this harder than the numbers suggest. Complete at least one 80+ km training ride with climbing to confirm your fitness and fueling strategy. Don't underestimate the accumulated fatigue of rolling coastal terrain.

non_negotiables.requirement: Understand the cause — ride with intention
non_negotiables.why: Amy's Gran Fondo is a memorial ride. Understand Amy Gillett's story, the Foundation's mission, and why cycling safety matters. The riders around you may have lost friends or family on the road. Ride with awareness, respect the pace groups, and be part of the community — this isn't a race to win, it's a ride to honor.
